content.text: Dependency upgrades are higher risk for agentic AI platforms than for standard web services because the dependencies that most often need upgrading — LLM client libraries, vector databases, prompt frameworks — have the highest potential for breaking agent behaviour in ways that standard regression suites do not detect. An upgrade window that is planned, executed with a canary deployment, monitored against a pre-defined set of rollback criteria, and supported by pre-tested rollback artifacts converts this high-risk activity into a predictable maintenance operation.

A scheduled upgrade window is a planned maintenance period, typically 2-4 hours, during which dependency upgrades are applied to a production system using a controlled deployment process: staging first, then canary deployment to a small traffic fraction, then full rollout. The window includes pre-defined rollback criteria (conditions that trigger an automatic or manual decision to revert) and pre-prepared rollback artifacts (the pinned package versions and data snapshots needed to revert without delay).

For agentic AI systems, the upgrade window process must include AI-specific steps that are absent from standard web service upgrade runbooks: capturing a baseline eval suite result before the upgrade (to detect post-upgrade quality regression), running the eval suite after staging deployment (to detect API behaviour changes before production), and restoring vector index snapshots if the vector database version changes the index format. The rollback plan must also account for in-flight agent sessions that may be interrupted if the upgrade changes the session serialisation format.

Rollback plans are not afterthoughts — they are prepared before the upgrade window opens, tested in staging to verify they work, and documented with the exact commands needed to execute. A rollback plan that has never been tested is not a rollback plan; it is a hope.

Unplanned dependency upgrades — triggered by a security patch SLA, a feature dependency requirement, or an automatic version bump from a tool like Dependabot — carry higher risk than planned upgrades because they happen without the preparation steps (baseline capture, pre-tested rollback, stakeholder notification) that a scheduled upgrade window provides. Planning the upgrade window converts the upgrade from a surprise to a controlled operation.

For LLM SDK upgrades specifically, the failure modes are subtle. An SDK upgrade from version 0.20 to 0.21 may change the default temperature parameter, rename a response field, or change the error handling for rate-limit responses. None of these changes will be caught by unit tests that mock the LLM client. The eval suite, which exercises the actual LLM interaction, will catch them — but only if the upgrade window process requires the eval suite to run before full production deployment.

Rollback plans matter because upgrades sometimes break production in ways that the pre-deploy testing did not catch. The time between the decision to roll back and the completion of the rollback is when the system is degraded. A pre-tested rollback plan with documented commands reduces this window from 30-60 minutes of scrambling to 10-15 minutes of executing a known procedure.
The staged deployment process (staging, canary, full rollout) detects problems at the smallest possible blast radius before they affect all users.
Running the eval suite before and after the upgrade catches LLM API behaviour changes that unit tests miss.
A rollback plan that has been tested in staging before the upgrade window opens can be executed in under 15 minutes under production pressure.
The pre-window notification and maintenance window announcement give users and internal stakeholders predictable expectations about service availability during upgrades.
The window runbook execution log, eval suite comparison results, and rollback artifact receipts constitute documented evidence of controlled change management for compliance audits.
A well-prepared upgrade window requires 3-5 hours of preparation work (baseline capture, rollback testing, stakeholder notification) in addition to the window execution time.

Scheduling upgrades in windows rather than deploying them immediately means MEDIUM and LOW severity patches wait for the next window. This must be weighed against the risk reduction from the controlled process.
Running the eval suite twice (before and after) for every upgrade window adds cost and time. For large eval suites, this may add 30-60 minutes to the effective window duration.
Even with graceful drain, multi-turn agent sessions in progress during an upgrade may be interrupted if the upgrade changes session serialisation format.
The upgrade window process has four phases: pre-window preparation (T-7 days), window execution (2-4 hours), post-window soak period (24 hours), and rollback (if triggered).

Pre-window preparation: the on-call engineer runs the full test suite and captures the eval suite baseline. Database and vector index snapshots are taken. The rollback requirements.lock file is prepared with the pre-upgrade versions and tested with a rollback deployment in staging. Stakeholders are notified via email and the status page.

Window execution: the upgrade is applied to staging first, followed by the eval suite comparison. If the eval suite passes, the change is deployed to 5% of production traffic (canary). The canary is monitored for 30 minutes against pre-defined rollback criteria (error rate, P95 TTFT). If the canary is healthy, the full rollout proceeds.

Post-window soak: for 24 hours after the full rollout, the team monitors the system against the pre-upgrade baseline and the rollback criteria remain active. User-reported issues and anomalous trends in the eval quality metrics can trigger a rollback even after the window closes.

attrs.alt: Upgrade window timeline: pre-window preparation, window execution with canary deployment, soak period, and rollback path with AI-specific steps

content.text: The timeline shows the four phases of the upgrade window process. The rollback path (dashed arrow) is triggered by rollback criteria during either the canary phase or the soak period. AI-specific steps (eval baseline, eval comparison, session drain, vector index backup) are called out separately below the main timeline.

Scheduled upgrade windows are appropriate for any dependency upgrade that affects production systems and carries non-trivial risk. In practice, this means: any upgrade to an LLM client library (anthropic, openai, litellm), any upgrade to a vector database client or server, any upgrade to a prompt templating or agent orchestration framework, and any dependency upgrade that has a HIGH or CRITICAL CVE.

Low-risk upgrades (MEDIUM or LOW CVE patches that do not affect LLM-related or vector DB packages, patch version bumps for logging libraries, test framework upgrades that do not affect production code) may be applied through the standard PR and deployment process without a formal window. The decision threshold should be documented in the team's upgrade policy.

Upgrade windows are not appropriate for hotfixes that need to be applied immediately (CRITICAL CVEs or production incidents). These follow the emergency change process, which has abbreviated review and deploys to production as quickly as testing allows.

# runbooks/dependency-upgrade-window.md

## Dependency Upgrade Window Runbook

### Pre-window checklist (T-7 days)

- [ ] Feature freeze confirmed with engineering lead
- [ ] Full test suite run — all green (run: `make test-full`)
- [ ] Eval suite baseline captured: `python scripts/eval/capture-baseline.py`
- [ ] Pre-upgrade system snapshots taken:
  - [ ] Database snapshot: `make db-snapshot TAG=pre-upgrade-$(date +%Y%m%d)`
  - [ ] Vector index backup: `make vectordb-backup`
  - [ ] Current requirements.lock committed and tagged: `git tag pre-upgrade-$(date +%Y%m%d)`
- [ ] Rollback requirements.lock prepared in `rollback/requirements.lock`
- [ ] Rollback tested in staging: `make test-rollback-staging`
- [ ] Stakeholder notification sent (see template: docs/upgrade-notification-template.md)
- [ ] On-call engineer confirmed and briefed

### Window execution

Step 1: Announce maintenance start
  - Post in #incidents channel: "Maintenance window open. Dependency upgrades in progress."
  - Update status page if applicable.

Step 2: Deploy to staging
  pip install -r requirements-upgrade.txt
  make deploy-staging
  make test-smoke-staging
  python scripts/eval/run-eval.py --env staging --compare-baseline

Step 3: Canary deployment (5% traffic)
  kubectl set image deployment/agent-api agent=gcr.io/project/agent:upgrade-candidate
  # Monitor for 30 minutes before proceeding

Step 4: Full production rollout
  Only if canary SLOs are healthy (error rate < 1%, P95 TTFT < 2000ms).
  kubectl rollout restart deployment/agent-api

Step 5: Post-deploy validation
  make test-smoke-prod
  python scripts/eval/run-eval.py --env prod --compare-baseline --alert-on-degradation

### Rollback criteria (auto-trigger or manual decision)

Roll back immediately if any of the following:
- Eval pass rate drops more than 3 percentage points vs baseline
- P95 TTFT increases more than 20% vs pre-upgrade baseline
- Error rate exceeds 2x pre-upgrade baseline for more than 5 minutes
- Any safety evaluation case fails that previously passed

### Rollback procedure

Step 1: Declare rollback decision
  - Post in #incidents: "Rolling back dependency upgrade. Reason: [state the criteria met]"
  - Page on-call engineer if not already active.

Step 2: Revert application
  pip install -r rollback/requirements.lock
  make deploy-staging  # verify rollback in staging first (if time allows)
  kubectl set image deployment/agent-api agent=gcr.io/project/agent:pre-upgrade-tag

Step 3: Restore data if needed
  # Only if vector index or checkpoint format changed
  make vectordb-restore TAG=pre-upgrade-$(date +%Y%m%d)

Step 4: Verify rollback health
  make test-smoke-prod
  python scripts/eval/run-eval.py --env prod --compare-baseline

Step 5: Post-mortem
  Required within 72 hours. Template: docs/upgrade-post-mortem-template.md
Canary health monitoring with auto-rollback trigger

# scripts/upgrade/canary-monitor.py
"""
Monitors canary deployment health and triggers automatic rollback
if any rollback criterion is exceeded.
"""
import time, requests
from datetime import datetime

PROMETHEUS_URL = "http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query"
ROLLBACK_CRITERIA = {
    "error_rate_multiplier": 2.0,  # 2x pre-upgrade error rate
    "p95_ttft_multiplier": 1.20,   # 20% increase in P95 TTFT
}

def query(expr: str) -> float:
    result = requests.get(PROMETHEUS_URL, params={"query": expr}, timeout=5)
    data = result.json()["data"]["result"]
    return float(data[0]["value"][1]) if data else 0.0

def monitor_canary(
    baseline_error_rate: float,
    baseline_p95_ttft: float,
    duration_seconds: int = 1800,   # 30 min canary window
    check_interval: int = 60,
) -> str:
    start = time.time()
    checks_passed = 0
    while time.time() - start < duration_seconds:
        canary_error_rate = query(
            'sum(rate(genai_requests_total{status=~"5..",deployment="canary"}[5m])) / '
            'sum(rate(genai_requests_total{deployment="canary"}[5m]))'
        )
        canary_p95_ttft = query(
            'histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(genai_ttft_seconds_bucket{deployment="canary"}[5m])) by (le))'
        )

        if canary_error_rate > baseline_error_rate * ROLLBACK_CRITERIA["error_rate_multiplier"]:
            return f"ROLLBACK: error rate {canary_error_rate:.2%} > {baseline_error_rate * 2:.2%} threshold"

        if canary_p95_ttft > baseline_p95_ttft * ROLLBACK_CRITERIA["p95_ttft_multiplier"]:
            return f"ROLLBACK: P95 TTFT {canary_p95_ttft:.2f}s > {baseline_p95_ttft * 1.2:.2f}s threshold"

        checks_passed += 1
        print(f"[{datetime.utcnow().strftime('%H:%M:%S')}] Canary healthy: "
              f"error_rate={canary_error_rate:.2%}, p95_ttft={canary_p95_ttft:.2f}s")
        time.sleep(check_interval)

    return f"PROCEED: canary healthy for {checks_passed} checks over {duration_seconds // 60} minutes"

# scripts/upgrade/rollback.py
"""
Executes the rollback procedure: reverts to pinned versions,
restores data artifacts if needed, and validates the rollback.
"""
import subprocess, sys, time
from pathlib import Path

ROLLBACK_REQUIREMENTS = "rollback/requirements.lock"
PRE_UPGRADE_TAG = "pre-upgrade-2024-11"   # Set before upgrade window

def revert_packages():
    print("Reverting packages to rollback requirements...")
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["pip", "install", "-r", ROLLBACK_REQUIREMENTS, "--require-hashes"],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"Package revert FAILED: {result.stderr}")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Package revert complete.")

def revert_deployment():
    print("Reverting Kubernetes deployment to pre-upgrade image...")
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["kubectl", "rollout", "undo", "deployment/agent-api"],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"Deployment revert FAILED: {result.stderr}")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Waiting for rollout to complete...")
    subprocess.run(
        ["kubectl", "rollout", "status", "deployment/agent-api", "--timeout=300s"],
        check=True
    )

def restore_vector_index_if_needed(format_changed: bool):
    if not format_changed:
        print("Vector index format unchanged — no restore needed.")
        return
    print(f"Restoring vector index from snapshot {PRE_UPGRADE_TAG}...")
    subprocess.run(
        ["make", "vectordb-restore", f"TAG={PRE_UPGRADE_TAG}"],
        check=True
    )

def validate_rollback():
    print("Running post-rollback smoke tests...")
    subprocess.run(["make", "test-smoke-prod"], check=True)
    print("Running eval suite comparison...")
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["python", "scripts/eval/run-eval.py", "--env", "prod", "--compare-baseline"],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    print(result.stdout)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print("WARNING: Eval suite did not return to baseline after rollback. Investigate.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    revert_packages()
    revert_deployment()
    restore_vector_index_if_needed(format_changed=False)
    validate_rollback()
    print("Rollback complete. File a post-mortem within 72 hours.")

Monthly maintenance window for an enterprise agentic AI platform: second Tuesday of each month, 02:00-06:00 UTC. All non-emergency upgrades are batched into this window. The window is announced 7 days in advance to internal stakeholders. The upgrade bundle for November included: anthropic SDK 0.20 to 0.21 (minor version bump with breaking change in streaming), chromadb 0.4.22 to 0.4.24 (security patch), and three standard library patches. The eval suite comparison after staging deployment caught the streaming format change in the SDK; the upgrade was paused and the agent's streaming parser was updated before proceeding to the canary phase.

Emergency window for a CRITICAL CVE: CVE in jinja2 (used for prompt templating) discovered Monday 09:00. Emergency window opened Monday 14:00 (5 hours after detection). Abbreviated pre-window: rollback requirements.lock prepared (30 min), staging deployment and eval suite run (45 min). No canary phase (CRITICAL CVE — abbreviated process). Full production deployment Monday 16:00. Post-window soak 24h. No rollback triggered.

A healthcare AI startup operating a clinical note-taking assistant had been applying dependency upgrades ad-hoc: a developer would bump a version, run unit tests, merge, and deploy. In three months, two production incidents were traced to dependency upgrades: one where an LLM SDK upgrade changed the default max_tokens parameter (causing truncated clinical notes), and one where a chromadb upgrade changed the embedding distance metric (degrading retrieval accuracy for related-note lookup).

The team implemented a monthly upgrade window with the following AI-specific additions to their standard runbook: capture eval suite baseline before every window, run eval suite after staging deployment for any LLM SDK or vector DB upgrade, and set rollback criteria at 3% eval pass rate drop or 15% retrieval recall drop. In the following 6 months, the canary phase caught two additional regressions (both caught in staging, not canary — the staging eval run was the most effective catch). The upgrade process went from two incidents per quarter to zero.
Rollback plan not tested before the window:
A rollback plan that has never been tested is likely to encounter unexpected errors under the pressure of a production incident. Test rollback in staging before every upgrade window.
Without a pre-upgrade eval baseline, there is no objective comparison point after the upgrade. The team cannot tell whether a 2% drop in pass rate is due to the upgrade or natural variance.
Canary too small or too short:
A 1% canary for 5 minutes does not provide enough data to detect a subtle regression. Use at least 5% of traffic for at least 30 minutes.
Not draining agent sessions before upgrade:

Upgrading a worker that is handling an in-flight multi-turn agent session may interrupt the session if the upgrade changes the session state format. Always drain active sessions before applying upgrades.
Rollback criteria too tight or too loose:

Rollback criteria that are too tight (1% TTFT increase) cause unnecessary rollbacks due to natural variance. Criteria that are too loose (50% error rate) miss real problems. Calibrate thresholds against 7-day baseline variance.
Fix the upgrade window to a recurring schedule:
Monthly windows at a predictable time (second Tuesday, 02:00 UTC) let stakeholders plan around them and let the team build routine discipline around the preparation process.
Applying multiple medium-risk upgrades in a single window is more efficient than multiple small windows and reduces the total number of production change events per month.
Automate the baseline capture and comparison:
The eval baseline capture before the window and the comparison after staging deployment should be automated steps in the window runbook, not manual tasks that may be skipped under time pressure.
Keep the rollback requirements.lock in version control:

The rollback artifact (the pinned requirements file for the pre-upgrade state) must be committed to version control before the upgrade window opens, so it is retrievable even if the deployment tooling is unavailable.
Write a post-mortem for every triggered rollback:
A rollback indicates that the upgrade process failed to catch a problem before production impact. Understanding why the catch failed improves the next window's preparation.

The upgrade window duration is dominated by the eval suite runtime and the canary soak period. A comprehensive eval suite of 200 cases may take 20-40 minutes; the canary soak adds 30 minutes. For a 2-4 hour window, this leaves 60-90 minutes for the actual upgrade application and deployment operations, which is ample for most upgrade bundles.

For teams with large dependency graphs and frequent upgrades, consider splitting the upgrade window into two tracks: one for LLM-related and vector DB upgrades (which require the full AI-specific validation process) and one for standard library patches (which require only unit tests and smoke tests). This reduces the window duration for low-risk patches while maintaining full validation for high-risk changes.
Tracks rollback criteria metrics during canary
Runs eval suite before/after with comparison output

The upgrade window runbook and rollback plan contain infrastructure details (kubectl commands, snapshot names, rollback procedures) that should not be publicly visible. Store them in an access-controlled documentation system, not in a public wiki or open repository.

The rollback requirements.lock should be signed with a team GPG key and the signature committed alongside the file. Verify the signature before executing a rollback to prevent a compromised artifact from being used.

Only the on-call engineer and their designated backup should have the credentials to execute production deployments during an upgrade window. Time-limited access tokens reduce the risk of credential misuse.
Every command executed during the upgrade window should be logged with the executing user identity, timestamp, and outcome. This log is the change record for audit purposes.
Even if no rollback is triggered, test the rollback procedure in staging every quarter to verify that the artifacts and procedures remain valid as the system evolves.

An upgrade window is like a scheduled surgery with a pre-op checklist, a surgical team ready to respond to complications, and a recovery protocol. The pre-window preparation is the pre-op: the patient (system) is assessed, the surgical plan is reviewed, and the emergency response (rollback) is staged and ready. The canary deployment is the surgical observation period — a controlled trial on a small portion before committing fully. The soak period is the recovery room: the patient is stable but monitored closely before being cleared.

The rollback plan is not used when everything goes well — but it must be ready and tested before surgery begins. A rollback plan that has never been tested is like an emergency kit that was packed years ago and never opened to check that all the components are still there.

Agentic AI upgrade windows must include AI-specific steps: eval suite baseline capture before the window, eval suite comparison after staging deployment, and retrieval quality validation for vector DB upgrades.
Rollback plans must be tested in staging before every upgrade window opens; an untested rollback plan is not a rollback plan.
Canary deployment to 5% of traffic for at least 30 minutes, monitored against pre-defined rollback criteria, is the most cost-effective way to catch regressions before full rollout.
Rollback criteria should be calibrated against 7-day baseline variance: too tight causes false rollbacks, too loose misses real regressions.
Fix the upgrade window to a predictable recurring schedule (e.g., second Tuesday of each month) so that preparation overhead is amortised into a routine discipline rather than a per-upgrade overhead.
Every triggered rollback requires a post-mortem that explains why the pre-deployment testing did not catch the regression.
